coredata.dc:description: Since the chiral emission of excited states is observed on carbon dots (CDs), exploration towards the design and synthesis of chiral CDs nanomaterials with circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) properties has been at a brisk pace. In this regard, the “host and guest” co-assembly strategy based on the combination of CDs and chiral templates has been of unique interest recently for its convenient operation, multicolor tunable CPL, and wide application of prepared CDs-composited materials in optoelectronic devices and information encryption. However, the existing chiral templates that match perfectly with chiral CDs exhibiting optical activity both in ground and excited states are rather scarce. In this work, we synthesize the chiral CDs that could induce the spontaneous supramolecular self-assembly of N-(9-fluorenylmethox-ycarbonyl) (Fmoc)-protected glutamic acid to form chiral hydrogels with helical nanostructure. The co-assembled hydrogels show powerful chiral template function, which not only enable chiral CDs with a luminescence dissymmetry factor (glum) up to 10-2, but also have universal chiral transfer to inserted dye molecules, realizing full-color CPL and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) CPL as well as the distinction between left and right circularly polarized light. This CPL-active template based on chiral CDs enriches the design scenario of chiral functionalized nanomaterials.

SEM images of (a) Fmoc-l-Glu/l-CDs and (b) Fmoc-d-Glu/d-CDs hydrogels; (c) CD spectra and (d) CPL spectra of Fmoc-L(D)-Glu/L(D)-CDs hydrogels.

(a) Schematic illustration of co-assembly between four typical dye molecules (ThT, TO, PY, TMPyP4) and Fmoc-L(D)-Glu/L(D)-CDs hydrogels template; (b) Normalized CPL spectra of ThT/TO/PY/TMPyP4 when co-assembled with the template.

(d) CPL spectra of ThT/(ThT + TO)/(ThT + TO + PY) when co-assembled with template.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the chiral emission of excited states is observed on carbon dots (CDs), exploration towards the design and synthesis of chiral CDs nanomaterials with circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) properties has been at a brisk pace. In this regard, the “host and guest” co-assembly strategy based on the combination of CDs and chiral templates has been of unique interest recently for its convenient operation, multicolor tunable CPL, and wide application of prepared CDs-composited materials in optoelectronic devices and information encryption. However, the existing chiral templates that match perfectly with chiral CDs exhibiting optical activity both in ground and excited states are rather scarce. In this work, we synthesize the chiral CDs that could induce the spontaneous supramolecular self-assembly of N-(9-fluorenylmethox-ycarbonyl) (Fmoc)-protected glutamic acid to form chiral hydrogels with helical nanostructure. The co-assembled hydrogels show powerful chiral template function, which not only enable chiral CDs with a luminescence dissymmetry factor (glum) up to 10-2, but also have universal chiral transfer to inserted dye molecules, realizing full-color CPL and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) CPL as well as the distinction between left and right circularly polarized light. This CPL-active template based on chiral CDs enriches the design scenario of chiral functionalized nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decade, as an important branch of functionalization of carbon dots (CDs), chiral CDs have been studied extensively not only due to their multicolor chiroptical characteristics, but also they maintain the advantages of good biocompatibility, low cost as well as easy to function of carbon-based nanomaterial [1]. The first example of zero-dimensional spherical chiral CDs was synthesized in 2016, with a size lower than 10 nm [2]. The applied research related to chiral CDs has been later broadened to many fields such as enantiomeric discrimination [3], asymmetric catalysis [4], chiral detection and biological effect [5,6,7]. Besides, the exploration of the chirality of chiral CDs from the ground state to excited state promotes the development of a completely new optical character of chiral CDs [8]. Synthetic efforts leading to chiral CDs with circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) have flourished, which greatly stimulated the evolution of practical applications in the field of chiral optics [9].

As the emission analogue of circular dichroism (CD), CPL is a phenomenon in which chiral luminescent substances spontaneously emit left- or right-handed circularly polarized light, and can be divided into left-handed CPL (L-CPL) and right-handed CPL (R-CPL) by the difference in intensities of left- and right-handed polarized emission (IL/IR) [10]. And similar to the absorption, a luminescence dissymmetry factor (glum) is introduced to evaluate CPL activity and is theoretically derived from the following Equation for most molecular systems:
g
lum
= 2 ×
I L

I R
I L

I R
= 4 cos θ
m

2
≈ 4 cos θ
m
μ
where |m| and |μ| denote the magnetic and electric transition dipole moments, respectively, and θ is the angle between them [11]. The above equation gives the exact direction of the design of novel CPL-active materials through rationally adjusting |m| and |μ| vectors of chiral molecules. This understanding encourages researchers to intentionally construct the chiral materials with helical, axial, planar, propeller or twisted molecular structure, which is favorable to induce polarized spin radiative emissive due to they are more likely to produce special helix of the electron arrangement, resulting in a larger magnetic transition dipole moment [12,13,14].

Although the glum value of most organic CPL systems tested in solution is generally between 10-5 and 10-3 [15], there are increasing strategies have been reported to further improve the glum, including supramolecular assembly/aggregation, liquid crystal, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), achiral component, triplet–triplet annihilation-based photon upconversion, plasmon resonance, intermolecular charge-transfer, machine learning-assisted rational design [16,17,18,19]. Generally, a typical CPL material contains two major moieties: a chiral part and a luminescent part, which directly leads to three typical models be proposed to guide further molecular design toward effective chiral templates in self-assembled supramolecular systems: i) chiral assembly of chiral luminescent molecules; ii) chirality transfer between the chiral molecules and the achiral luminescent molecules; iii) breaking of symmetry of the achiral luminescent molecules in nanoassemblies [16]. These chiral self-/co-assemblies with designable nano-/micro-structures could provide powerful approachs to precisely regulate the arrangement of building blocks and promote the amplified CPL signals during assembly process via various non-covalent interactions. Nowadays, CPL-active materials are increasingly attracting attention due to their high directivity, spatial resolution and sensitivity, exhibiting enormous potential applications in 3D displays [20,21], bio-probe [22,23], asymmetric synthetic catalysis and information encryption [24,25,26,13]. Particularly, chiral CDs-based CPL composites have come into prominence in optoelectronic devices and selective imaging recently [27].

However, although CDs with chiroptical activity in their CD spectra were easily obtained using chiral molecular precursors, their CPL activity was not easily expressed [28]. An emerging method is to employ atropisomers or enantiomers of amino acids as chiral precursors to achieve the intrinsic CPL properties of chiral CDs, while their glum value can only reach 10-4, which is far from being applied in practice [29,30]. Our group reported an innovative way that CDs with self-assembled nature can spontaneously form helical nanostructures leading to the expression of supramolecular chirality and CPL in CDs assemblies, but it was greatly limited by solvent environment and raw material characteristics [31,32]. To date, the most common strategy is to co-assemble CDs as luminescent guests with chiral template hosts, conferring CDs with tunable CPL through the transfer and amplification of chirality. However, existing chiral templates useful for doping CDs were still rare because the chirality matchability in composites was challenging. According to statistics so far, there are only cellulose and [33,34,35], perovskite nanocrystals [36], chiral glutamate and phenylalanine-derived gelators [37,38,39,40]. Therefore, developing more chiral templates is of great significance for exploring new designs of CDs-based CPL materials and their wider applications.

Herein, as shown in Scheme 1 , we reported a facile approach to synthesize a novel chiral template with highly chiral transfer efficiency for the CPL of chiral CDs and a wider range of fluorescent molecules. First, the auratus-emitting chiral CDs (L-/D-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method using amino acids as chiral precursors. And we unexpectedly found that they could function as key chiral inducers to trigger the co-assembly with Fmoc-Glu to construct chiral supramolecular hydrogels with helical nanostructures, enabling them the chiral emission of excited states and a maximal |glum| value of 0.01 meanwhile. In addition, various fluorescent dyes also expressed helicity at the nanoscale level when they were introduced into this template, thus leading to the full-color CPL and dual CPL-FRET. Then, chiral hydrogels were further processed into free-standing polymeric films to differentiate left and right circularly polarized light, revealing their potential to be used as chiral light source detectors. We envisage that our chiral CDs-based CPL template will bring a new platform for the design and application of CPL-active materials at nanoscale.

L-/D-Tryptophan (L-/D-Trp) (>98 %, AR), o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) (>98 %, AR), Fmoc-L/D-glutamic acid (>98 %, AR), polyvinyl alcohol (AR) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Concentrated H2SO4 (98 wt%, AR), Thioflavin T (ThT, AR), Thiazole Orange (TO, AR), Pyronin Y (PY, AR), meso-Tetrakis (4-(N-methylpyridiumyl)) porphyrin (TMPyP4, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were used without further treatment. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ·cm) from a Milli-Q automatic ultrapure water system.

The L-/D-CDs were prepared from L-/D-Trp and o-PD by solvothermal methods. L- or D-Trp (0.176 g), o-PD (0.125 g), 500 μL H2SO4, 10 mL of ultrapure water were added to a 25 mL autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 7 h. After the reactors were cooled down naturally to room temperature, the reacted solution was filtered with a filter membrane (0.22 μm) and then placed in a 1 KDa dialysis bag for three days and the water was changed every half a day to obtain purified L-/D-CDs. Lyophilizing the purified solution to produce a solid powder for later use.

All the co-assembled hydrogels were prepared in a closed glass vial (inner diameter 1 cm, height 4.5 cm). After concentration optimization experiment, a mixed solution of Fmoc-L-Glu (6 mM) and L-CDs (100 μg/mL) with a total volume of 2 mL ultrapure water was added to the corresponding marked sample bottle. The mixture was heated until the solid was completely dissolved and subsequently cooled down to room temperature under environmental conditions and incubating about 2 h. The formation of hydrogels was confirmed by a vial inversion test. Fmoc-D-Glu and D-CDs hydrogel was also prepared using the same method and ThT/TO/PY/TMPyP4-doped hydrogels were prepared by adding 0.5 mM corresponding dye molecules into the mixed solution of Fmoc-Glu and chiral CDs under the same method.

Typically, chiral composite films are prepared by adding 5 wt% polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to the Fmoc-L(D)-Glu/L(D)-CDs/PY mixed solution in a sealed glass vial, and heating the mixture solution under intense stirring until the solid is completely dissolved, then the solution was poured onto polystyrene Petri dishes (3.5 mm) and allowed to dry for 4 days under ambient conditions giving rise to free-standing film.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were carried out at room temperature on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD instrument manufactured by KRATOS corporation (Manchester, UK). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) analysis were carried out at room temperature on a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer manufactured by Thermo Fisher Scientific Corporation (Massachusetts, USA). Test samples were prepared by KBr tableting method and the spectrum was collected with 1 cm−1 resolution and 512 numbers of scan conditions in the range of 400–2000 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis were carried out at room temperature on a D2 PHASE X-ray diffraction system manufactured by Bruker AXS corporation (Karlsruhe, Germany). Samples of gels were all freeze-dried before XRD analysis and the XRD patterns were measured in 2θ range from 10 to 80 degrees with a step size of Δθ = 0.1 degree (Cu–Kα radiation, 40 kV, 40 mA, λ= 1.5418 Å). The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra (DMSO‑d 6, 400 MHz, r.t.) were recorded on a Bruker 400 NMR spectrometer. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were conducted at room temperature on a GEMINI 300 field emission scanning electron microscope with SE2 detector manufactured by Zeiss corporation (Oberkochen, Germany). All samples of gels were evenly spread on the conductive adhesive to form a thin layer and left to dry before scanning. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were conducted at room temperature on a Tecnai G2 F20 S-Twin TMP electron microscope manufactured by FEI corporation (Oregon, USA) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The CDs solution is dripped on the copper mesh to dry naturally before testing. Atom force microscopy (AFM) were recorded in the tapping mode with a commercial Dimension Icon scanning probe microscope from LabRAM HR Evo under ambient conditions. Fluorescence spectra were obtained at room temperature on a FS5 fluorescence spectrophotometer manufactured by Edinburgh instruments corporation (Edinburgh, UK) using a quartz cuvette with 1.0 cm path length. Both excitation and emission scanning slits are 1 nm and dwell time is 0.3 s. And the step size of both excitation and emission spectra were 1 nm.

UV–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained at room temperature on a Lambda 750 spectrophotometer manufactured by Perkin-Elmer corporation (Massachusetts, USA) using a quartz cuvette with 1.0 cm path length. Spectra were scanned from 200-800 nm with a step size of 1 nm at a scan speed of 100 nm/min, and the bandwidth for incident light beam is 2 nm. CD and CPL spectra were measured at room temperature on a J-1500 Circular Dichroism and a CPL-300 Spectrometer attached with a temperature controller manufactured by JASCO corporation (Tokyo, Japan) using a quartz cuvette with 1.0 mm path length, respectively. Samples were sandwiched between two 1 mm quartz plates. CD Spectra were scanned from 200-800 nm with a data pitch of 0.2 nm at a scan speed of 200 nm/min, and the bandwidth for incident light beam is 5 nm and D.I.T. is 2 sec. The measure range of CPL spectrum is 390–550 nm with a data pitch of 0.2 nm and an excitation wavelength at 350 nm at a scan speed of 100 nm/min, and the bandwidths for incident and emission light beam are both 16 nm and D.I.T. is 4 sec. Each CD/CPL spectrum was an average of three measurements and was repeatable. Optical and fluorescent microscopy images were taken at room temperature on a FV3000 microscope manufactured by Olympus corporation (Tokyo, Japan). Samples are uniformly dispersed on glass slides for imaging observation. Fluorescence imaging was performed at an excitation wavelength of 405, 488 and 561 nm respectively. All spectral measurements above were tested at least three times and were repeatable.

Among the various strategies reported for the synthesis of CDs, the protocol of preparing chiral CDs based on the hydrothermal method using non-chiral carbon-containing compounds as carbon sources and chiral amino acid molecules as chiral sources have evolved into a mature and efficient route after verification by numerous researchers over the years. The experimental results, theoretical analysis and related molecular simulation calculations have provided sufficient evidence to prove the effectiveness of this scheme [8,41,42,43,44]. Basically, Carbon source undergoes carbonization at higher temperature whereas chiral molecules such as amino acids act as the chirality inducing agent and the synthesized CDs were purified by adopting techniques like precipitation and dialysis [30,45]. According to the similar results of various experiments conducted along this route, the reported core–shell model of CDs could be adopted to explain chirality evolution in these materials.

Since it is well known that CDs are often described to consist of an electron-dense core covered by a residue-rich ligand shell [46]. And after these years of evolution, the formation of CDs with a core/shell structure can be summarized into 4 consecutive steps: (i) aggregation of small organic molecules, (ii) formation of a dense core with an extended shell, (iii) collapse of the shell and (iv) aromatization of the core. Especially, the unique fluorescence of CDs is achieved already after stage (iii), when CDs present the final core–shell structure [47]. In our work, we adopted same sythetic strategy, with the o-PD as carbon source to facilitate the polymerization of the core and selected the amino acid molecule-Trp as a chiral inducer to prepare L-/D-CDs with chiral ligand shells.

Since the difference between L-CDs and D-CDs is only apparent in their chiral optical properties, most of the characterizations described below use L-CDs as an example. As shown in Fig. 1
a and Fig. S1 a, Morphological, dimensional and crystallographic characteristics of the L-CDs were obtained by TEM. TEM images show that the chiral CDs are spherical-like nanoparticles with an average size of about 3–4 nm, and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of single L-CDs further demonstrate a typical lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [48]. Owing to the recent wide investigation, current theories point out that there are two types of cores of CDs, graphitic crystalline and amorphous cores, based on the degree of the presence of sp2 carbon in the core [49]. Among them, the graphitic crystalline core with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm has been more reported, consistant with previous results, in which AA-stacked carbon layer with interlayer covalent CC bons and functional groups on the surface [50,51,52]. The proposed model is also in good qualitative agreement with our TEM test results of L-/D-CDs, which is consistent with published majority of the CDs in TEM observations so far. The morphologies of the CDs were also further examined by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The images ( Fig. S2 ) showed monodisperse D-CDs with morphology heights below 5 nm and the result agrees well with the TEM characterization, again confirming the formation of carbon nanoparticles.

In addition to providing a direct way to confirm the presence of CDs by observing the graphene crystal structure in nanoparticles through HRTEM, XRD is also an important technique to help detect the morphology of CDs cores. The XRD pattern of L-/D-CDs as shown in Fig. S3 , there is a very broad peak in the range of 2θ angles between 20 and 40° degrees for Cu Kα radiation, consistent with a nanocrystalline graphitic structure, which means that some ordered layered nanocarbons with atomic structures close to graphite among fabricated mixes are present [53,54,55].

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed to evaluate the optical property of chiral CDs (Fig. 1 b and Fig. S1 b). As is known the UV/vis spectra of typical CDs have two typical absorption peaks at around 250 and 350 nm, which could be assigned to π–π* and n–π* transitions of sp2-hybridized CC and CO, respectively, in the core of CDs [56,57]. For the L-CDs, two distinct absorption peaks at 216 and 275 nm and a weak broad peak near 425 nm were observed respectively. The former can be assigned to the π-π* transitions of the CC and CN bonds in hybridized aromatic sp2 domains [58]. The photoluminescence (PL) of CDs is usually believed to originate from the radiative recombination of excitons in core (carbon-core states) and surface electronic states, molecular states, surface functional groups and surface energy traps. Among them, molecular states and carbon-core states were demonstrated for the first time by Giannelis et al. [59]. The trapping of excited state energy by the surface states at 425 nm results in an optimal excitation and emission wavelength at 410 nm and 575 nm of l-CDs respectively. Moreover, the L-CDs do not exhibit significant excitation-dependent PL behavior (Fig. 1 c), there is only a faint red-shift occurred as the excitation wavelength increased from 370 to 440 nm (Fig. S1 c), which may be due to the bright emission is a kind of surface defect-derived fluorescence, consistent with the analyzed model [45,60].

The surface information of chiral CDs and L-/D-Trp was further analyzed by XPS, as shown in Fig. S7 and S8 . The elemental analysis results show that L-CDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O. Deconvoluted XPS spectra for C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s show a C 1 s band containing four main peaks at 284.40, 285.91, 286.94, and 288.81 eV, attributed to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO, respectively. Three distinct peaks at 399.77, 400.92 and 402.12 eV can be observed in N 1 s, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. For the O 1 s band, two peaks at 531.85 and 533.28 eV are assigned to CO and CO, respectively. In contrast to chiral CDs, L-/D-Trp exhibit a peak of pyridinic N missing in the N1s band, which indirectly proves that the precursor undergoes polymerization. Based on high-resolution XPS measurement, for N-doped CDs the core is not purely composed of carbon [61]. Doped-N was also a contributor to the formation of core in cyclic form including pyrrolic, graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen resulting in a strong electron-withdrawing ability within the conjugated C plane and improving the PL by enhancing the core emission [62]. This is also consistent with the analysis of the CDs with a core–shell structure model, revealing that the L-/D-CDs have O, N-doped carbon networks in their cores, amide and hydroxy groups at their surfaces.

The chiroptical properties of chiral CDs were further characterized by CD spectroscopy. It is evident that L-/D-Trp led to the formation of L-/D-CDs with corresponding mirror image CD scans, as shown in Fig. 1 d. The chiral signals observed in 200–236 nm region for the L-/D-CDs originated from the intrinsic chirality of precursors L-/D-Trp (Fig. S11), which sometimes coincided with (or became dominated by) signals from aggregates that formed both at the surface or within the respective CDs [45]. While in the range of 237–310 nm, the CD spectra of L-/D-CDs extend and fluctuate symmetrically due to the carbonization and polymerization of the precursor, which is generated by the π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains in a chiral environment and is completely different from L-/D-Trp [68]. Such CD signals (bisignate intense peaks) were observed for nanoparticlesand are referred to as exciton coupling [69,70]. For a pair of chiral chromophores, their interaction results in a split of an excited level, whereas the ground state remains unchanged. This exciton interaction or exciton coupling generates two electronic transitions: from the ground state to α- and β-polarized excited states with opposite rotational strengths. In CD spectra, the appearance of intense extrema/peaks with opposite signs, which are referred to as bisignate Cotton effect [71].

As per the prescribed model, the inconsistency between the CD spectra of L-/D-CDs and precursor demonstrates that L-/D-CDs are composed of an achiral carbon core that is enclosed within an amorphous shell comprising fluorophores and conjugated structures which have abundant heteroatoms. The chiral nature of the structures could be attributed to the shell comprising undecomposed amino acids or their derivatives connected by amide bonds [72]. The hybridization of the electronic structure between the chiral residue in the shell and the achiral fluorophore units could lead to the generation of chiral hybridised states in the CDs [73]. More importantly, only the participation of L-/D-CDs can trigger the co-assembly process in the supramolecular self-assembly system of this work, since subsequent various spectral results and a series of control experiments have proven that neither L-/D-Trp nor organic fluorescent molecules can induce self-assembly with Fmoc-Glu and affect the properties of L-/D-CDs.

The nanostructure of chiral hydrogels was investigated in detail by SEM. Fig. 2
a and b show the helical nanostructures formed in the complex of Fmoc-Glu and L-/D-CDs. For the co-hydrogel of Fmoc-L-Glu/L-CDs, it exhibits a uniform-sized fibrillar morphology with distinct left-handed helices with a pitch of around 50 nm and tightly intertwined. In contrast, right-handed helical structure was found in Fmoc-D-Glu/D-CDs hydrogel. It is well known that natural Fmco-Glu lacks the ability to self-assemble to form regular nanostructures, and their aggregates produce agglomerations and precipitates in aqueous solutions [81,82]. The mirror image of the helix indicates that addition of corresponding chiral CDs to the enantiomer of Fmoc-Glu resulting in the opposite helical nanostructures. The formation of chiral hydrogels further demonstrated that molecular chirality of Fmoc-Glu and L-/D-CDs could be transferred to the macroscopic level by self-assembly between them and induce the expression of overall supramolecular chirality. Additionally, the chiral hydrogel was further characterized by rheological measurements. In frequency scanning mode ( Fig. S17 ), the storage modulus (G') of both is much greater than the loss modulus (G'') throughout the entire scanning frequency range, indicating a stable cross-linked network structure is formed, and G' gradually increases with the enhancement of frequency, which proves the typical viscoelastic behavior of hydrogels.[83] The average G' values of Fmoc-L-Glu/L-CDs and Fmoc-D-Glu/D-CDs are 19.5 kPa and 20.1 kPa, respectively, indicating that chiral hydrogels also have good mechanical properties.[84].

In order to further explore the microstructure information of the chiral hydrogels, their XRD and FTIR spectra were measured respectively. As shown in Fig. S3
a, compared to L-/D-CDs without obvious characteristic XRD peaks, both hydrogels had distinct peaks at 10.72°, 14.46°, 18.92°, 21.62°, 25.69°, which displayed calculated d-spacing of 0.82, 0.61, 0.47, 0.41 and 0.35 nm, respectively. The reported d-spacing of native Fmoc-Glu (2.45 nm) is shorter than the length of two Fmoc-Glu molecules (2.68 nm), indicating a bilayer structure of overlapping fluorenyl rings.[82] Therefore, the corresponding ratio of obtained d-spacings to that of Fmoc-Glu were 1:3, 1:4, 1:5, 1:6 and 1:7 respectively, which suggested a well-defined lamellar structure and revealed the π-π stacking of Fmoc moiety.[81] The almost consistent FTIR spectra (Fig. S3 b) of both hydrogels mean that they are bonded in the same way. Compared with the FTIR data of L-/D-CDs, the enhancement of stretching vibration peaks of Fmoc-L-Glu/L-CDs and Fmoc-D-Glu/D-CDs in the range of 3325–3218 and 1108 cm−1, which belonged to –NH and CO respectively. Besides, the bending vibration of CO at 1630 cm−1 increased and moved to 1710 cm−1 while the stretching vibration of −C = ONH at 1264–1450 cm−1 decreased, these changes indicate the formation of amide–amide H-bonds in the self-assembly process.[81] These results show that L-/D-CDs may interact with homochiral Fmoc-Glu through H-bonds and strong chiral dipole–dipole interactions (DDI) originated from their chiral morphologies to induce ordered π-π stacking among their multiple bilayers to form a regulated chiral aligned growth mode,[77] and further hierarchically self-assembles into higher level nanohelices causing the formation of hydrogels and chiral structures.[75] Therefore, the sterocentres may impose a chiral arrangement so that the electric transition dipole moments can couple. The coupled motion of the oscillators can now have circular component and hence the transition acquires also magnetic dipole character and becomes CPL active.[74].

Next, the chiral characteristics of the ground state of the co-assemblies were checked by CD spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 2 c. It is obvious that Fmoc-L-Glu/L-CDs exhibited negative and positive Cotton effects near 200 and 230 nm respectively, which were consistent with the CD signals of L-CDs and Fmoc-L-Glu in the range of 200–250 nm, indicating their molecular chirality was well transferred to the assembly. Interestingly, although Fmoc-Glu is a chiral molecule, the CD signal of Fmoc chromophores could not be detected in the range of 250–310 nm ( Fig. S18 ), implying molecular chirality could not be transferred to the Fmoc chromophore. While Fmoc-L-Glu/L-CDs showed typical Cotton effects in the region of 250–310 nm, which are accordant with the adsorption band of Fmoc-Glu, assigned to the π-π* transitions of the Fmoc moiety. This result supported that L-CDs induced chiral packing of Fmoc-L-Glu and expressed supramolecular chirality through self-assembly. In addition, Fmoc-L-Glu/L-CDs presented a broad negative Cotton effect between about 400 and 500 nm, corresponding to the n-π* transition belonging to the surface chiral chromophore of L-CDs near 425 nm. It was shown that the chirality of surface chiral functional groups was also transferred and amplified during the co-assembly process, which provided a basis for the chirality expression of chiral CDs excited states. More importantly, CD spectra of the chiral hydrogels exhibited symmetrical chiral signals, indicating their helical nanostructures are opposite and again proving chiral CDs could induce Fmoc-Glu to self-assemble into opposite helical nanostructures.

In order to further verify the versatility of the chiral hydrogels as a CPL template, we attempted to explore whether the FRET of CPL can be conducted among different inserted dyes by means of the chiral transfer. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence emission peaks of ThT and TO doped hydrogels overlapped with the absorption peaks of TO and PY doped hydrogels respectively, which meant that TO can not only become a good receptor for ThT but also act as a donor for PY, providing a necessary condition for FRET. When ThT and TO were co-assembled with the hydrogels simultaneously, it was found that the maximum CPL emission value of ThT at 490 nm descended with the increase in the proportion of added TO at the excitation wavelength of ThT, while a new CPL signal peak belonging to TO appeared and raised around 550 nm. When the second acceptor PY was continued to be incorporated on this basis, the CPL signal of TO diminished but a new emission peak attributed to PY arose near 600 nm, identifying there was efficient energy transfer from ThT to TO and then to PY. When the ratio of ThT/TO and ThT/TO/PY were 1:1 and 5:5:3, their energy transfer efficiency could respectively reach the maximum of 67 % and 52 %. The successful two-step CPL FRET between the three achiral dyes once again demonstrated the chiral hydrogels owned excellent chiral template properties and endowed the co-assembly with chiral expression of the excited state.

For the above prepared chiral helical supramolecular hydrogels, we wondered whether the efficacious chiral transfer process based on chiral template in gel phase could also be replicated in solid state, which is meaningful to expand its potential practical application in CPL photoelectric devices. Therefore, taking Fmoc-L(D)-Glu/L(D)-CDs/PY hydrogels as examples, we fabricated self-standing transparent composite films in 5 % PVA with a uniform thickness and the obtained films emitted orange yellow fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 5
a). Interestingly, both composite films responded in the CPL test, Fmoc-L-Glu/L-CDs/PY film displayed a R-CPL signal while Fmoc-D-Glu/D-CDs/PY film exhibited a L-CPL peak, and their maximum |glum| values are about 0.008 (Fig. 5 b and c). These results are close to the CPL characterization of corresponding chiral hydrogels and rule out the possibility of any artefacts due to linear polarization effects by measuring from different angles, which suggest that composite films retained the outstanding chiral transfer ability and chiroptical performance of template. The possible responsiveness of composite films under left- and right-handed circularly polarized (LCP and RCP) light irradiation was then explored as shown in Fig. 5 d. We were surprised to find that the emission intensity of Fmoc-L-Glu/L-CDs/PY film under excitation of LCP light was stronger than that of RCP light, indicating it can selectively transmit LCP light. While the blue shift of the emission center of composite films can be attributed to the constraints generated by the abundant hydrogen bonds between fluorescent molecules and PVA chains, which contribute to a stable surface state corresponding to high-energy and short wavelength emissions.[87] By comparison, for Fmoc-D-Glu/D-CDs/PY film with converse CPL characteristics, the fluorescence intensity excited by LCP was weaker than by RCP light, which meant it had a better selective transmittance to RCP light (Fig. 5 e). The selective discrimination of Fmoc-L(D)-Glu/L(D)-CDs/PY films to L/RCP light may originate from the discrepancies in chirality of excited states caused by different left- and right-handed helical nanostructures of chiral templates,[40] and this property also helped to offer a probable solution for the application of our chiral hydrogels as powerful chiral templates in CPL detectors.

In this work, the self-assembly process of Fmoc-Glu could be triggered by the corresponding L-/D-CDs to form supramolecular hydrogels with left-/right-handed helical architectures through synergetic contribution of H-bonding between the chiral CDs and Fmoc-Glu and the enhanced π-π stacking. The resulting co-assembled hydrogels are a brand-new CDs-based chiral template in addition to existing cellulose and perovskite nanocrystals,[33,34,35,36] chiral glutamate and phenylalanine-derived gelators. [37,38,39,40] And the CPL property of which can easily reach a highest |glum| value of 10-2, which is comparable to the majority of other CDs-based CPL active materials.[33,36,39,86] Further, they could work as a CPL matrix and transfer the chirality of excited states to dyes and composite films to achieve efficiently FRET-CPL and differentiation of LCP and RCP light respectively, confirming our chiral hydrogel is a preeminent CPL template and provide an innovative platform for the development of high-performance CPL functional materials.
